heading: 1 Introduction

heading: 2.1.1 Maxwell’s Equations
text:
Maxwell’s equations express, formally, properties electromagnetic waves.Gauss’s Law Electricity
\[ \nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_v \]Gauss’s Law Electricity
\[ \nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_v \]Gauss’s Law Magnetism
\[ \nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0 \]Gauss’s Law Magnetism
\[ \nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0 \]Faraday’s Law
\[ \nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t} \]Faraday’s Law
\[ \nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t} \]Ampere-Maxwell Law
\[ \nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t} \]Ampere-Maxwell Law
\[ \nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t} \]
- \(\nabla\) = Vector differential operator.
- \(\mathbf{D}\) = Electric flux density.
- \(\mathbf{B}\) = Magnetic flux density.
- \(\mathbf{E}\) = Electric field intensity.
- \(\mathbf{H}\) = Magnetic field intensity.
- \(\rho_v\) = Volume charge density.
- \(\mathbf{J}\) = Current density.
- \(\frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}\) = Displacement current density.

heading: 2.1.2 Stoke’s Theorem Physics
text:
Stokes’ theorem relates circulation vector field \(\mathbf{}\) around closed path \(L\) surface integral curl open surface \(S\) bounded \(L\) [1]:\[ \int_S (\nabla \times \mathbf{}) \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \oint_L \mathbf{} \cdot d\mathbf{l}\]:
* \(d\mathbf{S}\) surface element vector [1].
* orientation follows right-hand rule [2].

heading: 2.1.3 Stoke’s Theorem for Flux Integral
text:
\[\iint_S (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dA = \oint_C \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{r}'(s) \, ds\].,\[\iint_S (\text{curl} \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dA = \oint_C \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{r}'(s) \, ds\].formula:
* \(S\) piecewise smooth oriented surface space
* \(C\) piecewise smooth simple closed curve boundary \(S\)
* \(\mathbf{n}\) unit normal vector \(S\).
* \(\mathbf{r}'(s)\) unit tangent vector \(C\).
* \(s\) arc length \(C\).
* \(\mathbf{F}(x, y, z)\) continuous vector function

heading: 2.1.4 Surface Integrals and Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
text: \[\int_D d\omega = \int_{\partial D} \omega\]\(\omega\): differential \((k-1)\)-form.\(d\omega\): exterior derivative form \(\omega\).\(D\): \(k\)-dimensional domain integration.\(\partial D\): oriented boundary domain \(D\).

text: Changes electric field cause changes magnetic field. According special relativity, magnetic field actually electric field distorted motion electrons.

heading: 2.3 MATHEMARICS APPENDIX
text:
Stoke’s TheoremLet \(S\) piecewise smooth oriented surface space, let boundary \(S\) piecewise smooth simple closed curve \(C\). continuous first partial derivatives domain containing \(S\), :\[\iint_S (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dA = \oint_C \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{r}'(s) \, ds\].,\[\iint_S (\text{curl} \times \mathbf{F}) \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dA = \oint_C \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{r}'(s) \, ds\].formula:
* \(\mathbf{n}\) unit normal vector \(S\).
* \(\mathbf{r}'(s)\) unit tangent vector \(C\).
* \(s\) arc length \(C\).
* \(\mathbf{F}(x, y, z)\) continuous vector functionFluxTotal amount field fluid passing surface.Double IntegralThe double integral defined following identity:
\[\iint_R f(x, y) \, dA = \lim_{m, n \\infty} \sum_{=1}^m \sum_{j=1}^n f(x_{ij}^*, y_{ij}^*) \Delta \].
* \(R\) closed bounded region \(xy\)-plane function integrated [1, 2].
* \(m\) \(n\) number subrectangles region \(R\) partitioned [3, 4].
* \(R_{ij}\) smaller subrectangles resulting dividing \(R\) using grid lines parallel axes [4, 5].
* \(\Delta \) represents area subrectangle \(R_{ij}\) [4, 6].
* \((x_{ij}^*, y_{ij}^*)\) arbitrary sample point chosen within subrectangle \(R_{ij}\) [4, 6].
* limit taken \(m\) \(n\) approach infinity maximum diameter (greatest distance two points within subregion) approaches zero [3, 7].
* function \(f(x, y)\), integrand, typically assumed continuous \(R\) ensure limit exists independent choice sample points [7, 8].Double Integral Notational VariationsThere exist double integral two equivalent notations:
* Differential Area Form: \(\displaystyle \iint_R f(x, y) \, dA\).
* Coordinate Form: \(\displaystyle \iint_R f(x, y) \, dx \, dy\).Vector Differential OperatorThe vector differential operator expressing partial differentiation vector\[\nabla = \frac{\partial}{\partial x} \mathbf{} + \frac{\partial}{\partial y} \mathbf{j} + \frac{\partial}{\partial z} \mathbf{k}\]\(\nabla\) vector differential operator, commonly referred del nabla.\(\nabla\) vector differential operator, commonly referred del nabla.\(\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}, \text{ } \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\) partial derivatives.\(\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}, \text{ } \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\) partial derivatives.\(\mathbf{}, \mathbf{j}, \text{ } \mathbf{k}\) standard basis unit vectors pointing positive directions \(x, y, \text{ } z\) axes, respectively\(\mathbf{}, \mathbf{j}, \text{ } \mathbf{k}\) standard basis unit vectors pointing positive directions \(x, y, \text{ } z\) axes, respectivelyCurlLet \(\mathbf{v}(x, y, z)\) differentiable vector function expressed Cartesian coordinates components \([v_1, v_2, v_3]\), : \[\mathbf{v} = v_1 \mathbf{} + v_2 \mathbf{j} + v_3 \mathbf{k}\]
curl \(\mathbf{v}\) (also denoted \(\nabla \times \mathbf{v}\) \(\text{rot } \mathbf{v}\)) defined following symbolic determinant: \[\text{curl } \mathbf{v} = \nabla \times \mathbf{v} = \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ v_1 & v_2 & v_3 \end{vmatrix}\]DivergenceDifferentiation Vector\[\mathbf{v}'(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \0} \frac{\mathbf{v}(t + \Delta t) - \mathbf{v}(t)}{\Delta t}\].Vector Function\[\mathbf{r}(t) = \langle f(t), g(t), h(t) \rangle = f(t)\mathbf{} + g(t)\mathbf{j} + h(t)\mathbf{k}\]
- \(f, g, h\) components \(\mathbf{r}(t)\)

* \(R\) closed bounded region \(xy\)-plane function integrated [1, 2].
* \(m\) \(n\) number subrectangles region \(R\) partitioned [3, 4].
* \(R_{ij}\) smaller subrectangles resulting dividing \(R\) using grid lines parallel axes [4, 5].
* \(\Delta \) represents area subrectangle \(R_{ij}\) [4, 6].
* \((x_{ij}^*, y_{ij}^*)\) arbitrary sample point chosen within subrectangle \(R_{ij}\) [4, 6].
* limit taken \(m\) \(n\) approach infinity maximum diameter (greatest distance two points within subregion) approaches zero [3, 7].
* function \(f(x, y)\), integrand, typically assumed continuous \(R\) ensure limit exists independent choice sample points [7, 8].Double Integral Notational VariationsThere exist double integral two equivalent notations:
* Differential Area Form: \(\displaystyle \iint_R f(x, y) \, dA\).
